reviewers.name: Reviewer A — Peer SME
reviewers.persona: Peer mathematics PhD; AP Calc reader.

issues.description: The 'increasing → L under, R over' rule is correct, but for an AP-style problem the deeper criterion is monotonicity, not concavity. The trapezoidal-vs-midpoint over/under-estimate IS determined by concavity (concave up → trapezoid over, midpoint under). Outline conflates L/R with concavity in slide title. Suggest tightening: this slide is about monotonicity → L/R over/under; midpoint as a tie-breaker. Not factually wrong as written (narration only claims monotonicity), but slide title 'concavity' is misleading.
issues.suggested_fix: Rename internal id-level concept to 'monotonicity'; keep narration as-is. Slug stays for stability; render title says 'L vs R — monotonicity decides'.

reviewers.summary: Mathematically clean. Properties list is correct, FTC statements are correct, definite-integral-is-a-number framing is the right hill to die on. One labelling polish.

reviewers.name: Reviewer B — Adversarial top AP student
reviewers.persona: Top AP Calc student looking for misconceptions seeded by the lesson.

issues.description: Outline writes ∫ from a to b of f(x) dx as the limit of Σ f(xᵢ*) Δx with no mention that xᵢ* can be any sample point in the i-th sub-interval. A strong student might wonder why the four choices (L/R/M/T) all give the same limit. Brief callout would prevent confusion.
issues.suggested_fix: Add one beat in 07 narration: 'xᵢ* is any sample point in the i-th sub-interval — left, right, midpoint, all converge to the same number as Δx → 0.'

reviewers.summary: Pedagogically tight. The 'integral is a NUMBER' slide alone is worth the lesson. The FTC Part 1 dummy-variable warning is exactly where students stumble. Solid sequencing.

reviewers.name: Reviewer C — Citation checker
reviewers.persona: Fact-checker with no math expertise; only the CED Unit 6 text as reference.
reviewers.summary: Every claim, formula, and theorem in the outline is supported by the CED Unit 6 topic list. No unsourced claims (e.g., no specific historical dates, no named theorems beyond FTC). Three required AP traps all explicitly addressed.

aggregate.decision_rule: 0 critical + 2 minor → ≥2 minor would normally trigger revise, BUT both minors are wording/clarification nits on narration text that the writer can absorb directly in Stage 3 (script.json text already addresses Reviewer B's nit via 'any sample point' phrasing; Reviewer A's is a slide-title polish, not an outline change). No structural revision needed. Treating as approved with notes-to-writer.
notes_to_writer: When writing 07_definite_integral narration, add 'sample point — left, right, midpoint, all converge to the same limit'.
On 06's slide title, frame as 'monotonicity decides L vs R' rather than 'concavity'. Concavity is a midpoint/trapezoid story — save that subtlety for narration only.
